Lessons with the Masters In this intense discussion, you'll hear from David Deutch, one of the world's leading copywriters and experts on creative thinking, as Clayton drills him on his secrets for coming up with one brilliant idea after another. David shares lessons and secrets that have made him indispensable to clients, and few writers today have a better grasp on uncovering the big idea that will make a promotion successful. Follow his advice and your writing can't help but improve. For more on David, visit www.copyquickstart.com. Now, let's listen in. David, it's great to have you with us today. It's great to be here. I'd like to get started by having you tell us a little bit about your life growing up and your family life, your education. Give us some background, where you came from. Well, I came from New York City, born in Brooklyn. My parents were both social workers, and all their kids went into business. Although my sister recently went into social work after her career as a product manager. So I thought that was kind of interesting. Wow. Yeah, I think it's something she wanted to do from the beginning, but just didn't feel she was ready for it. Well, she went the opposite direction many of us have gone. That's right. That's what I feel like going into something else, I'll tell you. So I grew up in New York, went to Starbusson High School, went to school in the college in Binghamton, Harper College, which was part of the state university system. And after I graduated, I did some teaching jobs, and finally decided I'd like to earn a little more money than teaching could provide. And the main skill I had was being an incredibly fast typist. So one of the things I did was to get some temporary jobs typing, and then I found myself in the typing center at Ogilvy and Mather. And I figured, well, as long as I'm here, surely I might be able to write advertising copy that I'm typing up. So I applied to do that and started writing copy for Ogilvy and Mather, which was of course a great training ground. Ogilvy and Mather is kind of like a teaching hospital of an ad agency. Sure. What year was this? Oh, this was back in 75. Wow. Did you ever get to meet David Ogilvy? Yes. Yes, I mean I... That would have been a trip. Yeah, he was quite a character. I mean, he wouldn't have been able to tell you who I was from a lineup, but you know. His influence was certainly pervasive there. So your only qualification was you were a great typist? Yes, yes. And I was sort of an English major in college, although I technically majored in music. Had you done any sales work, taken any sales jobs during college or before you ended up at Ogilvy? You know, minor stuff of, you know, maybe I sold greeting cards a little bit once and had a newspaper route. You know, it's certainly something that I always wish that I had done more of. And sometimes I feel like I should just go out and get a job in a telemarketing center for a week or two just to get that experience. Just handling objections. Exactly. Right. Exactly. And just hearing what people say. Well, tell us some of your first experiences at Ogilvy. I can't imagine being a copy cup there and submitting copy to people with such extensive experience. It must have been intimidating. Yeah, it was. It was. Although, you know, pretty soon you get the hang of it. And, you know, nothing can certainly compare to the experience that we've had, you know, working with Dan in terms of, you know, of something being so totally critiqued. Mm-hmm. But. So after Ogilvy, you worked with Rosenthal? No, no, I worked with other ad agencies. Uh-huh. And, you know, I was everything. I was a copywriter. I never liked just doing copywriting, so I would, you know, for a while I would be an account executive. I would be a director of marketing. I would do some media stuff. I loved going after new business. That was the most fun thing to go. Me? Yeah. Yeah. Just pitching an account and getting excited. Everyone gets geared up and you do a presentation and you win it or you don't win it. It was just wonderful. But. There was your opportunity to handle objections. Yeah, really. Really. Yeah. So you, you know, you got that experience, whether it was before Ogilvy or during, after you still had that experience. I'm sure that contributes a lot to the strength of your copy today. Yes, I think it does. But anyway, one day I was, you know, minding my own business in an ad agency reading Success Magazine. And there was an ad by J. Abraham, four pages long, of course, taking up half the magazine about, you know, all this stuff that you had to know about marketing. And I was in marketing and I had to know this stuff, you know, how it is by the time you're done with the J. Abraham piece. You just, you have to order it. Yes. And I sent the $500 or whatever it was. I think it was $500 for the first thing, which is just a huge amount of money, you know, in that day for me to spend on an info product like that. And read his stuff and was, of course, just totally taken by it. Eventually I wound up paying $5,000 for the, you know, complete your marketing genius at work course or whatever it, you know, whatever it was, the step up from there. And decided I wanted to do that. I want to, you know, go out and, you know, write copy and do J. Abraham type deals. And I linked up with, you know, John Finn and he got me linked up with Jim Rutz. And before I knew it, I was so swept up into the whole, you know, world of writing copy that I forgot about doing J. Abraham deals where, you know, you make a client a dollar and he gives you a quarter of it. That was my fantasy of doing that. But you couldn't be, you know, getting the check right up front for doing copywriting. So the cash flow kind of won out there. I'm amazed at how incestuous this business is, you know. My office was next door to J. Abraham's office in 1975. And after I left that small agency there, Jim Rutz, followed me at that agency as their copywriter. Oh, wow. Was that Craig Huey's agency? Yeah. Yeah, okay. Yeah. And so, you know, we know a lot of the same people. Well, what happened next? Well, I worked with Jim Rutz for a year or two. What did you do for Jim? Worked with him on writing packages. Sometimes it would be, you know, he would have an idea. I would have an idea. And write the copy. He would, you know, he would edit it and make it more, you know, Jim is just wonderful at making copy interesting. He just refuses to let it be boring. Uh-huh. And, you know, that demand really helped me. You know, I've become a big believer in working with someone. I just don't think that there's any, you know, good as all the courses and your courses and that everyone's stuff out there is. There's something about actually working under someone, you know, having them throw their copy back at you and saying, try again, you know. Uh-huh. And seeing them write, seeing the process that they go through when they're on the phone or in person with you, when they're trying to come up with something. How do they think? What do they, you know, what do they review? What are the process that they go through in their head? It constantly amazes me. I can really pour myself into a piece of copy and feel like it's flawless. It's absolutely perfect and that nobody in the world could possibly improve on it and show it to somebody who just simply looks at it from 10 degrees off of where I was. Right. And they'll end up giving me four or five new ideas that strengthen it measurably. Uh-huh. Uh, we always encourage, you know, our writers to make contacts with other writers through the total package, through the forum, through the blog, and through the Easy Writers Club, and to set up these little copy review cells. So if you don't have an incredibly, incredibly good fortune to work with Jim Roths or Gary Bencevanger or Dan Rosenthal, you still have that constant refining process going on in your career. You know, that's a really good point. That really is, in a lot of ways, a substitute for that. You know, giving it to a couple of people can really almost be just as good, if not as good, because they will see things. They'll tell you it's boring. They'll tell you it's not convincing them. Uh-huh. Or they'll come up with objections that you can overcome. Uh-huh. Okay, so you're working with Jim. What happened next? Well, eventually Jim and I, you know, Jim, you know, encouraged me to go off on my own. And I did and started working with Boardroom and working with Phillips. And they would give my name to other people. So I would, you know, I worked with Rodale and people like that. And, uh, just, you know, it took off from there. You know, I think, uh, in the beginning it was sort of a struggle to, you know, there was a struggle to kind of figure out what is it that makes a package of control. You know, what are those things that can make you sure, or at least, you know, more sure that it's going to be a breakthrough package. And, you know, fortunately I was lucky enough to have some successes in the beginning where eventually I was able to kind of catch on, okay, this is what, you know, this is what does it. And it was nothing that I probably couldn't have told you verbally before then, but I think it just has to get into your bones. Does that make sense? It sure does. Yeah. It's between learning and doing. Yeah. It's the application of what you know and then being flexible enough to bend it and even break it sometimes depending on the market, the timing, the product and so forth. You know, some of the best writers, interesting you say that, a quality of some of the best writers that I know is courage. They just have this, the courage to, and sometimes the courage to be honest and just say what the book is really about. Yep. Whereas my tendency might be to say, well, I'm going to cover that up a little bit. You know, they're like, no, you know, just say, you know, this is this, you know, and the courage to try different ideas. You know, Jim Rutz especially will come up with the most off the wall things. You know, Ed Elliott who did a lot of design for Jim. Mm-hmm. By the way, for people who are listening, Jim is one of the four or five or six copywriters that I would say is definitely in the pantheon of living copywriters. We've all heard a lot about Gary Benzavenga simply because Gary kind of went public and started his own easing. Jim and Gary are right at the top along with Dan Rosenthal and a handful of others of people who really made the 70s and the 80s and 90s huge for Phillips and Boardroom and Rodale and all the usual suspects. Mm-hmm. Ed Elliott was his designer for a while and Ed told me a few months ago that we were talking about Jim and he says, you know how all of us have that little good angel on one shoulder and that little bad angel on the other shoulder? And they're constantly telling you, you know, what's appropriate, what isn't, what's good, what's bad, what isn't. And telling you that you can't do certain things. Well, somebody shot Jim. They told him he couldn't do certain things. And Jim breaks all the rules. One of my favorite examples of that is read this or die. A package for the health markets that Jim wrote. And the headline was read this or die. And here we are talking to old people and all laboring under the assumption that any discussion of death in our copy to old people who already had one foot in the grave would definitely kill readership. And Jim just went in and broke that rule with impunity and got a huge control from it. Yeah. One of my favorites is Hillary milking the cow. Have you ever seen that one? No. It was a package he did for a money publication of boardrooms and it had a, the picture was Hillary and she was milking this cow and the udders were kind of, you know, stretched. And she was really, I mean, milking the cow, very cartoonist picture. And it was something like, you know, Hillary made a, remember the cattle futures? Yes. And the whole thing, and it was about, you know, Hillary made a fortune, you know, on cattle futures. You know, it was, it was utterly legal, but, you know, it was this or that. And then, you know, here's how you can also make a lot of money. It just, it just sort of segwayed into what the newsletter was about by using that little current event thing. That's cool. But it was so attention getting. Yeah. Topicality sells. People were hearing about the Rose Law firm and the, and the cattle futures scandal and he tied it in. It's amazing. That courage, you're right. Courage is just amazing. You have one shot to make it and it takes, it takes big Cajones to step out there and say, I'm going to do something totally unexpected and completely unusual, you know, rather than following, following some, some established pattern or, or, or a formula. Right. It occurs to me too, though, that you had, you've had some really great breaks. I mean, let's face it. You, you, you stumble into a job at Ogilvy Mather and, or Mayther, as you pronounce it, and, and get to meet the great man himself, David Ogilvy. You, then you go from there and you work with J. Abraham, who's no slouch when it comes to copy and marketing. Probably one of the best. And then, and then Jim Rutz. But I think you had another thing in your favor when you went out in your freelance career. And that was the fact that you had been doing account work for ad agencies. So you were used to knocking on doors and going in and pitching clients. That had to help when you were trying to get your first clients as a freelancer. Yeah. I mean, that's a good point. I never really thought about that. It's a very similar thing, selling ad services, selling copywriting services. You know, one of the biggest challenges I think young copywriters face is that they're, they're terribly intimidated to go through that process. They love learning about copy and doing the courses, reading the books and, and doing the, the spec assignments and doing all this other stuff. But at some point, you know, you have to put on your suit, button it up and knock on a client's door and say, here I am, hire me. I think that's another area where courage comes into a crazy career. Definitely. I mean, when you say breaks, you're, I mean, you're absolutely right. You know, I was able to say, okay, I worked at Ogilvy and Maider. I've worked with Jim Rutz. You know, so that's a tremendous advantage to be able to do that. Absolutely. Beyond, beyond the lessons that we've all already learned so quickly, breaking the rules, having courage, putting yourself out there and making contacts. Are there any other, are there any other things to which you would attribute your success? Yeah, you know, I think, I think I work hard. I'm sort of driven to make something as good as it can be and for it to be successful. And I think that's real important with copywriting. You know, sometimes I'll work with a copywriter and I'll see that I can only push them so far. You know, they're not going to put the effort into this that I would put into it. They're not going to rewrite it 16 times like John Carleton, you know, did for that promotion he talks about. I mean, he says that with a lot of promotions actually. You, there's something about just hard work, about putting in the hours, about studying copywriting, about analyzing packages that I think is important. And I, something that, you know, I think that I have and it has been a tremendous advantage. Yep, effort is genius, huh? Yeah, really, 99% perspiration or whatever it is. That's right, 1% inspiration. It's so true. I love telling the story that I was at my desk at four o'clock one morning and I got an email from Gary Benzavanga. Oh yeah, yeah. I responded immediately and so it's almost like being in a chat room, you know. I responded immediately and he wrote back and he said, what are the chances that two of the world's top copywriters would be at their desk at this hour? Right. You know, and that's, yeah, I've observed the same thing. And you have an incredible reputation on our market. Dave, why don't you tell us some of the successes you're proudest of? Okay, well I think one of the first successes that I really still am proud of is there was a piece for a book boardroom had called the Encyclopedia of Estate Planning. And, you know, the challenge of making something like an Encyclopedia of Estate Planning interesting and, you know, promoting it to people was pretty incredible. I got the book, I looked at it and I said, how in the world do you, you know, do you make this interesting? Do you make people want to buy this book on? Of course, the world thinks it's estate planning. As you said, no one wants to talk about death anyway. Right. And then just the subject matter itself is so dry of a book like that. I remember a movie once where these guys, it was a prison movie and if you were caught doing something terrible, they made you spend a week in the cooler with an insurance salesman. That's very funny. To me it's the same kind of thing as estate planning. You know, it's like, oh my God, I'd rather have my toenails pulled out one by one than think about estate planning. How in the world did you make that interesting? You know, you stare at anything long enough and look at it, you can get interesting fascinations from it. I had a little bit of an advantage in that the package before mine, which was fatiguing after seven years, whatever, was a Mel Martin package for boardroom. And so I had a good kind of example in front of me. But, you know, even the most dry thing like a whole discussion about what do they call it, joint wills or something like that. And the pros and cons of joint wills. I remember taking that and turning it into a fascination that said, why if you have a joint will with your spouse, you should rip it into pieces right now. That's strong. So you can always, you know, you just have to find the drama in things. You just have to bring it home on a human level. And that gets back to that effort thing. You know, so many times with a book, you know, I've gone back to a page. Well, I thought I got all the fascinations from it. And I go back to it and then I look at it and there's one, you know, that just pops out at me the second or third time I look at it, that page. Do you remember what the headline was for that package? Everything your spouse wants you to know, would like you to know about estate planning in Perenn, but the IRS hopes you never find out. That's great. Was it a number 10 package? Yeah, just a simple envelope package and the control really was the one where it just said, oh, just something about very personal and confidential on the cover. And that was the Mel Martin package? No, no, no, that was mine. Oh, that was your... Yeah, that was what became the control. And then the headline, that particular headline then was just the headline and the letter inside the...what your spouse doesn't want you to know. But it seemed to work better with just a confidential on the front, which of course with estate planning, when you open it up, you don't get that ayuk reaction about, yeah, they just said it was confidential. I mean, estate planning really is something that's confidential. Right. Right. Good. So it was credible. Right. Yeah. Give us some other examples. I was pretty proud of helping launch Michael Murphy's high tech investing newsletter. That was huge. For Phillips. Yeah, that was a huge...turned into a huge success, that newsletter. And that, you know, I pretty much used to be what your broker isn't telling you about high tech investing. You know, high tech was just becoming very well known. Everyone wanted to get into high tech. Everyone thought they should be in high tech. So the idea that you were not being told things about your broker at that time was very appealing. And it took off like a rocket. I remember that one. Mm-hmm. Yeah, I did some work on his premium service on the back end. Oh, okay. The BioTech. Yeah, the BioTech. Yeah. Right. I launched that service for them. So that's cool. Well, there was a package of yours I held up as a shining example at the summit last year. I'm trying to remember which one it was. Oh. Is it a boardroom book package? Or was it that tax one? I think it was in...wasn't it a newsletter package where you were citing a whole bunch of the very top analysts? Oh, yeah. Yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah. For Invest with the Masters. Invest with the Masters. Yeah. Tell us about that package. Oh, gosh, you're asking me to go back in time here. I hope no one puts me on the spot like this. Yeah. That was a package for Agora. And they had acquired that newsletter, Invest with the Masters, which was basically, you know, advice from different master investors like Warren Buffett. And Peter Lynch. And, you know, Sir Templeton. And I wish I could remember the headline now, but, you know, the bullets, I think the fascinations were really what helped that a lot. Your fascinations were powerful. And your use of the...you were an expert name dropper on that cover. Yes. Had credibility at the Wazoo. Yeah. It was just great on so many levels. I remember one of them was why Peter Lynch is investing in gold funds. Yeah. You have to read that. You have to. You have to read that. Yeah. I love that package. I think I have to make myself a note here to get a copy of that so we can make a PDF and show it to people. Okay. Any other successes you want to talk about? Anything that springs to mind? The stuff I've done with the Wylan sisters for Boardroom. I helped launch their health book that they did. I don't know if you know who they are. They're two sisters who write books on health. And they also did a household hints book for Boardroom. And they're just great reporters. They're great at finding out little tidbits of information. They're very into alternative health and alternative cures. I think their grandmother was, you know, was like an herbalist. And I did the packages that successfully launched their health book for them as well as the one that launched the household hints book. Do you remember your headlines? Oh boy. The household hints one was, honey, why are my new shoes in the freezer? And that was, you know, because when you, what you do is, and it sounds so totally bizarre. You take a bag of water and you put it in the shoe. So it like fills up the shoe. When you put it in the freezer, the water in the bag expands as it freezes and stretches out the shoes a little bit nicely. And so putting the shoes in the freezer just nicely stretches out the shoes. That's beautiful. Again, you got to read that. And then the one for the health book was homegrown cures and kitchen cures and homegrown miracles or something like that. Which was a, just a nice general headline for summing up their book, which was kitchen cures. It was stuff that you could do from stuff you had in the kitchen and homegrown healing miracles or something like that. Well, you know, I've been hearing a name for years and years and years and years and I was so delighted that you were at the summit last year so I got to meet you. Tell us a little bit about how copywriting has changed your life. Well, I think on a couple of levels, you know, in a lot of different ways. One is just monetarily being able to afford things. I would never have been able to afford if I had relied on my limited, you know, skills in other less lucrative areas such as typing or, you know, management or something like that. You know, it's led me to business opportunities like creating my own product, you know, joint ventures with people. Knowledge wise, you know, in terms of what I've learned about copywriting, about writing, about psychology, learning the subjects I've had to learn about to write the packages like health and investing. You know, I now, like you, I now know a tremendous amount about health. Sometimes I think I may know a little too much because, you know, you kind of know 20 things that'll work for arthritis or you know, you know, you know why it may work, why it may not work. This sometimes work, this person says this, this person says that. And the friendships that I've made with other writers like yourself, with even dare I say clients, and just, you know, in terms of self-knowledge in the struggle to improve, you know, as you know, you can't improve writing without kind of improving yourself as a person. Because to improve writing, you need to learn things like focus, or at least I did, needed to learn things like how to focus, how to concentrate, how to be empathetic, how to suppress critical judgment while I'm writing. So I just feel like in so many ways it's, you know, it's helped me grow. You know, I don't think I've ever heard anyone put that into words before, but that concept of suppressing critical judgment is so important. Violencing that voice, that critic inside that stops you from going down or tries to stop you from going down all the roads you need to go down to find the right way to do this. Right. And in fact, I have a copy cub who, and it's not John Newtson, it's not our in-house copy cub here. It's another guy, but he's learned so much about copywriting, and he's seen so many incredible examples. Now imagine you're a young copy, you know, when you and I came up, there were no total packages or bens of anger bullets or AWAs, and you just learned by reading other people's stuff. And you learned while you were actually doing. Now people are going through these courses and amassing all this information, and by the time they sit down to write their first real-world assignment, they've read Lies, Lies, Lies, and, you know, 12 Smiling Swindlers and Forbidden Cures and all of these other packages that have mailed in the tens of millions. And they sit down and they write a line and everything they write seems to pale next to the great examples they've seen. And so this inner critic is just beating them up. And this one copy cub, I'm thinking of, absolutely freezes him. How do you silence that inner critic? How do you do it? Is it a mere act of will? Well, it's an act of will. It's tricks. You know, you just, you force yourself to say, for instance, you know, I am going to just write without stopping. I am just going to write at full speed. It's something called free-writing, actually, where you just write. And no matter what it is, you know what I mean? You have to suspend the idea that it's going to be good. You really have to give yourself permission to, you know, to write bad copy. And just say, you know, I'm just going to write, really write whatever comes out. And just as an exercise, you know, until you can develop that ability to not go back. And then, of course, the tricks you can do with the keyboard, I think Joe Vitale actually writes and covers up his screen sometimes. So he can't look at what he's writing and go back and edit it. I quite often, you know, my approach is I absorb everything I can about the prospect. I absorb everything I can about the product. And this is over days. And then I get up early one morning and I go down and without looking at any notes, I just lean back, close my eyes, put my hands on the keyboard and start thinking about how the prospect and the product connect. Right. And I just, it's a brain dump. You call it free writing. To me, it's a brain dump. And just everything that goes through my mind comes out my fingers. And I could end up with 10, 15, 20 pages of stuff that it's amazing how much of it is usable and insightful and creative and, you know, really on target. So you're actually writing copy when you do that. Well, I'm not requiring myself to be writing copy. I'm just, I'm kind of free associating. You know, I'm allowing my subconscious to feed up to me what it's been chewing on over the last few days as I was internalizing everything I know about the prospect and the product. You know, what are the prospects needs, frustrations, desires and fears? And, you know, who is he? What's he thinking? What's he keyed into right now? What's he worried about? And so I've immersed myself in that. And then I know the product inside out. So I go through that process of just kind of allowing my mind to feed stuff out that I can then dump on paper. And a lot of what comes out is just, it's not going to be used, but maybe it helps me focus or draw into sharp focus how to talk to this prospect. What's the tone I should use? You know, what's appropriate? Which of these benefits do I feel like I need to really offer up first and then second and third? So it's just a very helpful exercise. It's kind of getting out of the way of your own subconscious, or of your own conscious mind, and allowing your subconscious to just ramble. And I found some of my best ideas that way. So it's interesting. The more I talk to copywriters, the more I find that the really great ones naturally evolved very similar approaches to writing. Right. Yeah. You can't write with the brakes on. You just can't, no matter how good you are. And then you use the analogy of driving, and it's true. You don't go down the road, you should go down, and you're driving with the brake on when you try to edit like that, as you write. You know, there's another aspect of that too. So many people write in and they want to know about compliance issues and legal issues and what they can say and what they can't say. That's like driving down the road with the brake on. Right. You know, my advice is always forget that there are compliance issues. You're not a lawyer. That's not your job here. A lawyer will look at this and make sure it's okay. You're a copywriter. And so, you know, ignore the rules. Ignore what, you know, the FDA says or ignore what the SEC says. Eventually your copy will be brought into compliance, but if you're focused on that, you're not going to be focused on writing powerful copy. Right. Yeah. And the same is true really for even what the product does or doesn't do. You know, if you kind of write it as if the product does the most incredible thing that the prospect wants and you've at least got that on paper, then you can try to find a way to bring that closer to the reality of what the product is. But you're at least starting from that place now and you're going downward. It's a lot easier to go down than it is to go up. Right. That's true. It's like the law, second law thermodynamics, you know. It's always easier to write longer copy and cut it. It's always easier to write high-toned copy and tone it down. And then going the other way with it. Well, we were talking really about the things to which you've attributed your success. And I think you've given us a couple of great ideas here. What other thoughts do you have on that? Well, I think one of the things that I think has really helped me a lot is the whole idea of systematizing creativity. I don't think I'm the most naturally creative person in the world. I'm not one of these people that, at least I don't think, comes up with 100 billion ideas, you know, for something and then the problem is choosing them. And a while back I found that there are ways of making creative, accessing your subconscious, as you say, coming up with ideas, making it more systematic in terms of the questions that you ask yourself. And you've created a product thinking inside the box. Think inside the box, right. Yeah. That really helps with that. Tell us a little bit about it. Well, it's actually the idea of the questions are contained in boxes. And so it's these boxes that you can kind of look in and ask yourself the questions in them. And it triggers creativity. It triggers, you know, stuff that's in your mind anyway, but you have no way of getting to it. Like, for instance, the first box is the idea of adding a new dimension. Can you add a dimension of sight or smell or touch or movement, you know, to it? And so I'm thinking about coming up with an idea for something and I think about that box, you know, I might think of how do I add the dimension of sensory elements, you know, to it. So I might come up with a headline like, you know, what will your neighbors say when they see your new green lawn if it's a lawn care product, just to use an extreme example. So I'll bring the element of hearing into it. I'll bring the element of sight into it that the person reading it, you know, I'll have them envision something. So it's just a way to kind of trigger things that it's hard to get to if you're just kind of thinking in general, what would be a good idea for this product? What would be a good idea for this headline? What's a good idea for a headline? It's a great approach. It's a great approach. Systematizing creativity. It just sounds contradictory, doesn't it? Yeah, it really does, but, you know. Like, planning spontaneity. You know, if you've ever watched, I mean, I'm sure you've watched Jay and what he does. Jay has a very, Jay Abraham has a very specific list of questions that he asks himself about a marketing product, about a marketing project. You can see him going, is there any way I can do an endorsed mailing for this? Is there, what are the undervalued assets that this client, you know, that this client has? And you know, he goes through that, maybe even not consciously at this point, but certainly unconsciously. You know, he's going through that checklist. I think we all do that. It's just that our checklist can be improved. And I think your, I think, think inside the box really is a vast improvement. I tell my kids all the time, you know, there's only one Mozart born every three or four hundred years, but millions of people can play the piano. And you don't have to be born with an incredible creative, to be incredibly creative. And it's just a matter of learning or acquiring these tools for developing creative thought. And I think your book, your course, does that extremely well. Well, thank you. I appreciate that. You know, Moshe Feldenkrais said something interesting once. He said that the thing about geniuses is not so much that there, I mean, Mozart obviously was just wired differently, but normal geniuses. The thing about them is maybe not so much that they're so much better or so different or whatever, but they have found a way of doing something that works for them. Uh-huh. And you know, I think that's a lot of times what it is, is that, like when you talk about writing copy, you found the way of writing copy that works for you. Right. And you know, that's to a large extent why you're such a great writer. If you had never found these ways of doing it, if you were still trying to do it the wrong way or trying to do it in ways that didn't work for you, you would never have lived up to that potential. If somebody had told me... Let's see, the roles were reversed. And instead of Paris Lampropolis being my copycut back in the 90s, what if I had become his? And what if Paris had told me that, no, you have to begin writing the way I do, you have to write 500 fascinations for this product before you do a single other thing? Well, that's Paris' process. He does that because it works for him. Exactly. I try to do that. I'd have been suicidal. This wouldn't have worked for me at all. So that's so true. And you know what, David, that is really a part of why it's so important to, at some point, transition from the learning phase of your career to the doing phase. Yeah. Even if you don't have an active client, sit down, take a product somebody else is promoting, and write a better promotion piece. Do the work. You know, you could show the promo to the company that's promoting that product, and they may buy it from you. But even if they don't, you've had this experience out of the virtual world and into the real world. Right. That's where so many lessons are learned. So why don't you take it through your mental processes? How do you begin work on a new project? Can we skip the part about total panic and anxiety about how am I ever going to create something for this? Oh, I think it's instructive to know that that happens to you even after all these years. Well, you know, there's always that moment, I think, when you're starting, where you just kind of go, wow, what have I taken on here? And I think for me, a lot of the, when you talk about the mental stuff that I go through on a project, a lot of it is just getting myself into the right state, getting myself enthusiastic about the product, confident in my knowledge about the product and the market. I mean, like you, I'm really a believer in, if you can't sit, then I think you may even have been the one that articulated this so well. You have to be able to sit down with someone and explain to them with no notes, no nothing, why it is they should buy this product and convince them articulately. And if you can't do that, you can't sit down with your notes and pass promotions and everything else and write a convincing package that has to be in your head. That's absolutely right. It's just got to be in there. And so that's the first thing, is to become that knowledgeable, that enthusiastic and that confident that I'm going to produce a blockbuster control. Next thing I'll do is to think about the subject and about the market. You know, really just sit down and think about it. It's kind of something I learned from Kent Comey, because I would like talk to him and he would tell me like 20 incredible insights into this market that I was working with. And I would go, how does he do that? And I realized he must sit down and think about it. Why don't you do that? And so I've gotten in the habit of just sitting down and thinking, okay, what do I know about this market? What do I know about the person that would buy this product? You know, this person is afraid, this person wants this, this person thinks this or whatever. What do I know from my own knowledge and my own experience? You know, the next thing I think about is, you know, what are the objectives that I want the copy to do? You know, there have been enough times where I've written copy and spent, you know, all this time writing copy and realized at the end, oops, I kind of forgot that I wanted the copy to do this. Because, you know, as you know, it's not just a matter of, you know, I want the copy to make him pick up, open his wallet and pay money. You know, you say it, you want the copy to, you want to convince the person to read the copy, you want the person to think a little differently about this, you want to overcome certain objections. So I really make myself sit down and say, okay, what are the things I want this copy to do? So I don't wake up when I'm done with the copy and say, oops, I forgot to, you know, take this into account. Great. What else? Thinking about the audience, you know, just sitting down and thinking about who the prospect is, you know, what they think, even becoming the prospect, becoming a 60-year-old woman with arthritis, or becoming a, you know, a 60-year-old investor with, you know, $2 million invested in all these stocks and funds. And just think, well, what am I feeling? What am I seeing? What am I seeing on CNN? How do I feel about it? That's great. I do all of those things too. Absolutely essential. And then the last mental thing, I guess, that I do is really try to envision the leader's reaction to my copy. You know, try to picture them reading it and seeing what their response is. Is there stuff that they're objecting to? Is there stuff that they don't believe and I have to add more proof? I think that ability to read your own copy, as if someone else wrote it, is a really essential, you know, ability. And maybe one of those things that all, you know, the great copywriters have is just to be able to look at it and say, you know, this is how the reader is going to read it and that's not right, you know? This is not doing what I wanted to be doing. I'm struck by how much of your approach really is introspective. You know, you talked about silencing the inner critic. You've talked about, you know, telling yourself it doesn't have to be good, it just has to be black ink on white paper, you know, on your brain dump, on your free writing exercise. So much of this is just, is really introspective. I think you've identified, into words, in ways that no one I've ever talked to has before, some of the really deep things that we do without even thinking about them after all of these years. And I think this is one of them. Yeah, well thank you. I think a lot of it, and maybe a trait of good copywriters too, is just, you know, that studying of what other people do and, you know, how they do it. But this kind of Stanislavski method. Right, method copywriting? Yeah, well, well it's basically what you're doing is, first you've cast yourself in the role of the prospect before you write, mentally, playing that role. Okay, I'm a 60 year old woman with arthritis. How do I feel about that? You know, what do I fear? What do I want? What frustrates me? And getting into that role, right? But then you turn around and you just put yourself in the passive role of a reader, and once again you're playing a part. And now you're just the reader who got this thing in the mail, and you're reading through it, and you're looking for different things then, aren't you? You're looking for where the copy's slow, where it's fast, where it's credible, where you need a proof element, and you're looking at it from an entirely different standpoint, but you're still playing a role there. And that's the internal stuff I'm talking about. Right. Being able to, somewhere behind your face, you know, take on another persona and just change your perspective on the copy. And I think that's really good stuff. In fact, I'll probably steal your ID and write an article. Okay. Okay, so if we're going to make it as freelance copywriters, the one thing that we need is clients. And some clients, you both know, are just wonderful, and some of them are a royal pain in the ass, and most of them are somewhere down the middle. What, and there are clients listening to this, and they want better output from their copywriters. They want better quality copy. They want it faster. What can clients do to help us to do a better job and to do it faster? Well... Or conversely, what shouldn't they be doing that slows us down in a week in their copy? Right. Well, first of course, let me say that all the clients I work with are absolutely wonderful and have no faults. But seriously, I mean, the big mailers like Boardroom and Phillips, you know, KCI Healthy Directions, as you know, they know how to work with writers. Yes. And how to treat writers and treat us with the respect that we so totally deserve. You know, I think there's one thing that all clients could probably do better, and that's to tolerate discomfort more. Because I think often the best packages are the ones that make clients just a little bit nervous. If they're a little nervous about mailing it, if they feel like, oh, maybe that's a little strong, you know, should we really say that? And conversely, when a client really loves something that I've written, I get a little nervous. Because a lot of times they love it because it resonates with their deep understanding of the product. And the prospect doesn't have that deep understanding. So they feel the idea just perfectly sums up the newsletter or the book or whatever so perfectly captures the essence of it. But that's not what's going to win over the prospect. Right. And... I've developed the same superstition over the years. Yeah. If the client loves it, it worries me if he's a little uncomfortable. I mean, imagine for a moment if a raw copycuff with maybe only a couple of minor successes under his belt had gone into healthy directions and written a headline, Read This or Die. If it hadn't been Jim Rutt's, that would have never been mailed. And so getting out of the comfort zone is absolutely essential. And I think it's interesting that the person who ultimately, and I don't know who it was, ultimately approved that headline and said, okay, we're going to put some money behind this and test it. Right. The person who did that didn't do it because they had an innate sense that that headline would sell. They did it because they knew Jim Rutt's history of hitting grand slams. Right. And that's the wrong reason, isn't it? Because when the younger vibe comes in with something equally powerful, you're not going to want to test it because it's challenging. Yes. I mean, there is that aspect of it. There's also the aspect of it that the inside of the package was brilliant. Every page of that, I mean, you know that piece probably as well as I do, every page of that package is brilliant. Yes, absolutely. And the subhead, you know. Yeah, the subhead. What is it? You know, good news travels slowly. I mean, what a great subhead that was. Good news travels slowly. And the whole idea that medical news isn't leaching you because it travels so slowly. It takes 20 years for discovery to make its way into the clinical practice of your doctor. And on the back, he said, after you read this, you probably won't die of heart disease. Right. Right. You had this long list. It was just, I thought it was amazing. And everywhere in the world, somewhere in the world, the disease that you're worried about has been cured. Yeah, there's already a cure. Right. Maybe it's in a rainforest in the Amazon. Maybe it's in Belgium at a lab. But somewhere it's been cured. And this newsletter brings all that together. You know, so much of what we do is focus on the words. You know, we think of copywriting as writing. And as we talk about that package, it's clear. And so many of these other packages that work well. But it's not the words. It's the thought behind the words and the propositions behind those words that intrigue and really make reading the copy mandatory. You know, a lot of times I just want to say to writers when I'm reading their copy, stop writing. Right. You know, you're like, you're writing like I'm writing a headline. This is what I want my headline to say. You're not staring into the eyes of a prospect and talking to them in a way that's making their eyes light up. I think that's fantastic insight. So let's get practical here for a moment. Okay. When you worked with Ogilvy, you were probably on starvation rations. And today you're one of the most highly paid copywriters in America. How did you get from there to here? What are the things that you've done to increase your income along the way? Well, I think certainly, you know, working with Jim Rottz, we talked about before. Getting a mentor? Getting a mentor. Persisting. You know, being willing to work for, you know, on royalty. You know, that was, you know, Jim was always encouraging in terms of that. He said, you know, clients are more willing to pay you more money on the back end in total, you know, than they are willing to pay you upfront because it's, you know, they're not risking. I'm not articulating that very well. But, you know, they'd rather pay you a million dollars for something that works than pay you, you know, $50,000 for something that doesn't work. That's absolutely correct. And they pay you with a smile on their face if you're making them money. Some more so than others. Yes. That's certainly true. Most of the good ones, the big ones, you know, they're very happy to, you know, it's when you're working with someone that isn't used to doing that, that you really have to ask, are they going to happily write that check? Yeah. When, you know, this piece does as well as I'm expecting it to do. Well, I think we're just about out of time, David. You've given us so many really good insights today. And I appreciate the time that you spent with us on the phone. Well, sure. It was interesting talking with you. I really enjoyed it. And Dave lives at the bottom of the hill that I live on. I've been to Smoky Mountains and he lives in the, down at the bottom of that hill. Yep. Yep. And we're going to be at the top of the hill. So we're going to be getting together soon. And maybe when you're up here, we could do something else together to really help our people out. That'd be fun. I'd enjoy that. And for any of you who are interested in David's course, which really is excellent on creativity, think inside the box. Just drop us an email to our feedback box. Feedback at MakepeaceTotalPackage.com and we'll send you full information on it. So again, Dave, thanks a lot. Okay. Thank you, Clayton. Take care. You too. Bye. Bye-bye.
